perturbation.original_text: Contractor understands that the Company may terminate this Agreement for any reason at any time with 10 days prior written notice to Contractor.
perturbation.changed_text: Contractor understands that the Company may terminate this Agreement for any reason at any time with 2 days prior written notice to Contractor.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the notice period for termination to 2 days contradicts California law regarding reasonable notice for contract termination.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Labor Code Section 2922 states that an employment, having no specified term, may be terminated at the will of either party on notice to the other. While this seems permissive, case law interprets this to require 'reasonable' notice, particularly for independent contractors where a shorter notice period may be deemed unconscionable or a breach of implied contract terms. 2 days may be deemed unreasonable.

EMPLOYMENT RELATIONS [2700 - 3122.4] ( Division 3 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) CHAPTER 2. Employer and Employee [2750 - 2930] ( Chapter 2 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) ARTICLE 4. Termination of Employment [2920 - 2929] ( Article 4 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) 2922. An employment, having no specified term, may be terminated at the will of either party on notice to the other. Employment for a specified term means an employment for a period greater than one month. (Amended by Stats. 1971, Ch. 1607.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the notice period for contract termination from 10 days to 2 days. The Perturbation Explanation states this contradicts California law regarding reasonable notice. The Law Explanation elaborates that California Labor Code Section 2922 requires 'reasonable' notice, and 2 days may be deemed unreasonable. Scraped Law Snippet 1, citing California Labor Code Section 2922, states that an employment with no specified term can be terminated with notice. While the law doesn't specify a minimum notice period, the explanation argues that case law interprets this to require 'reasonable' notice, making a 2-day notice potentially non-compliant, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Also, Contractor hereby assigns, and agree to assign, to the Company all Inventions conceived or reduced to practice by Contractor within one year following the termination of our work for the Company (voluntary or otherwise), if the Invention is a result of the Company’s information which was obtained by Contractor during our work for the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: Also, Contractor hereby assigns, and agree to assign, to the Company all Inventions conceived or reduced to practice by Contractor within two years following the termination of our work for the Company (voluntary or otherwise), if the Invention is a result of the Company’s information which was obtained by Contractor during our work for the Company.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the invention assignment period to two years post-termination may be challenged under California law as an unreasonable restraint of trade.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally prohibits contracts that restrain anyone from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. While there are exceptions, a two-year assignment of inventions conceived after termination is likely to be seen as an unreasonable restraint, especially given the broad language about 'Company's information'.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the invention assignment period from one year to two years post-termination. This modification potentially violates California Business and Professions Code Section 16600, which generally prohibits contracts that restrain someone from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. Scraped Law Snippet 1 directly quotes BPC 16600(a): 'Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void.' The extended assignment period could be viewed as an unreasonable restraint of trade because it limits the contractor's ability to use their inventions for a longer duration after leaving the company.

perturbation.original_text: The exclusive jurisdiction for any legal proceeding regarding this Agreement shall be in the courts of said state, and Contractor hereby expressly submits to the jurisdiction of said courts.
perturbation.changed_text: The exclusive jurisdiction for any legal proceeding regarding this Agreement shall be in the courts of Scottsdale, Arizona, and Contractor hereby expressly submits to the jurisdiction of said courts.
perturbation.explanation: Forcing exclusive jurisdiction in Arizona courts may be unenforceable if the Contractor is based in California and the contract was substantially performed in California, as it could violate California's public policy and deny the contractor the right to sue in their home state.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 410.42 limits the enforceability of out-of-state forum selection clauses in certain contracts, particularly when they involve California residents. While this section pertains to construction contracts, the principle it embodies—protecting California residents from being unfairly forced to litigate disputes in distant forums—is a broader public policy concern that courts consider when evaluating the enforceability of forum selection clauses in other contexts. Changing the forum to Scottsdale, Arizona, might be unenforceable.

OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. JURISDICTION AND SERVICE OF PROCESS [410.10 - 418.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1969, Ch. 1610. ) CHAPTER 1. Jurisdiction and Forum [410.10 - 410.70] ( Heading of Chapter 1 amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 615, Sec. 3. ) ARTICLE 2. Forum [410.30 - 410.42] ( Heading of Article 2 amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 615, Sec. 4. ) 410.42. (a) The following provisions of a contract between the contractor and a subcontractor with principal offices in this state, for the construction of a public or private work of improvement in this state, shall be void and unenforceable: (1) A provision which purports to require any dispute between the parties to be litigated, arbitrated, or otherwise determined outside this state. (2) A provision which purports to preclude a party from commencing such a proceeding or obtaining a judgment or other resolution in this state or the courts of this state. (b) For purposes of this section, “construction” means any work or services performed on, or materials provided for, a work of improvement, as defined in Section 8050 of the Civil Code, and for which a lien may be claimed pursuant to Section 8400 of the Civil Code (whether or not a lien is in fact claimed) or fo
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Article 2. Forum - Sections 410.30-410.42 :: California Code of Civil Procedure :: 2005 California Code :: California Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of the California Code 2024 California Code 2023 California Code 2022 California Code 2005 California Code (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2005 California Code of Civil Procedure Sections 410.30-410.42 Article 2. Forum CODE OF CIVIL PROCEDURE SECTION 410.30-410.42 410.30. (a) When a court upon motion of a party or its own motion finds that in the interest of substantial justice an action should be heard in a forum outside this state, the court shall stay or dismiss the action in whole or in part on any conditions that may be just. (b) The provisions of Section 418.10 do not apply to a motion to stay or dismiss the action by a defendant who has made a general appearance. 410.40. Any person may maintain an action or proceeding in a court of this state against a foreign corporation or nonresident person where the action or proceeding arises out of or relates to any contract, agreement, or undertaking for which a choice of California law has been made in whole or in part by the parties thereto and which (a) is a contract, agreement, or undertaking, contingent or otherwise, relating to a transaction involving in the aggregate not less than one million dollars ($1,000,000), and (b) contains a provision or provisions under which the foreign corporation or nonresident agrees to submit to the jurisdiction of the courts of this state. This section applies to contracts, agreements, and undertakings entered into before, on, or after its effective date; it shall be fully retroactive. Contracts, agreements, and undertakings selecting California law entered into before the effective date of this section shall be valid, enforceable, and effective as if this section had been in effect on the date they were entered into; and actions and proceedings commencing in a court of this state before the effective date
perturbation.justification: The modified text specifies Scottsdale, Arizona as the exclusive jurisdiction, which could contradict California's public policy if the Contractor is based in California and the contract was substantially performed there. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation state that California courts may find such a forum selection clause unenforceable. Scraped Law Snippet 1, CCP 410.42(a)(1), states that a provision in a contract between a contractor and subcontractor with principal offices in California that requires disputes to be litigated outside of California is void and unenforceable. This supports the determination that the modified text creates a contradiction.
